For Indian cricket fans, who watched Pollard terrorise bowling attacks in the IPL for over a decade, this milestone carries particular resonance. It is a reminder that T20 cricket, a format India helped popularise, continues to produce history-making moments that transcend any single league or country.
key_facts: Pollard breaks Chris Gayle's all-time T20 run-scoring record
Pollard's tribute: 'Sorry, Universe Boss'
Both players are West Indian T20 legends with deep IPL history

stories.headline: ICC makes landmark ruling: Motherhood and cricket can coexist, maternity protections formalised
stories.what: The International Cricket Council has announced a landmark policy affirming that motherhood and professional cricket can coexist, introducing formal protections for women cricketers around maternity. The move is being seen as a watershed moment for women's cricket globally, acknowledging that female players should not have to choose between starting a family and continuing their international careers. The ICC's intervention brings cricket in line with progressive employment standards that several other sports have already adopted.
stories.lens: For Indian women cricketers — many of whom play under BCCI contracts — this ICC move could directly shape how maternity is handled at the highest level of the game, potentially changing careers and lives.
stories.article:
The International Cricket Council has taken a landmark step for women's cricket, formally acknowledging that motherhood and playing the sport at the highest level are not mutually exclusive. India Today reported the ICC's policy move, which introduces structured protections for women cricketers around maternity — a long-overdue recognition of the realities faced by female athletes.

The policy shift reflects a broader global reckoning in sport about gender equality and the conditions under which women compete professionally. For years, female cricketers have faced an unspoken ultimatum: prioritise their career or start a family. The ICC's intervention signals a formal rejection of that binary.

The move has been welcomed across the cricketing world as a meaningful step rather than a symbolic gesture. By embedding maternity protections into ICC frameworks, the governing body is pushing member boards — including the BCCI — to align their own contracts and policies accordingly. Several cricketers from various nations have in the past either retired early or taken extended breaks without adequate support structures.

For Indian women's cricket, which has grown dramatically in profile and commercial value over the past decade, this ruling could have immediate practical implications. As the BCCI expands investment in the women's game — including the Women's IPL — ensuring that top players are not forced to choose between family and career becomes not just an ethical imperative but a strategic one for the long-term health of the sport.
key_facts: ICC formalises maternity protections for women cricketers
Policy affirms players need not choose between motherhood and career
Move affects all ICC member boards including BCCI

stories.headline: Cocktail 2 Crosses Rs 76 Crore Globally in Three Days
stories.what: Shahid Kapoor's Cocktail 2 has grossed Rs 76.25 crore at the global box office in its first three days of release, setting a strong pace for a mainstream Bollywood commercial entertainer. The collection marks one of the more robust opening weekends for a Hindi film in 2026, underscoring continued audience appetite for star-driven theatrical releases. The film's performance will be closely watched as an indicator of whether post-pandemic box office momentum is consolidating.
stories.lens: A Rs 76-crore global opening in three days signals that big-budget Bollywood releases with established stars can still reliably pull Indian audiences back into cinema halls.
stories.article:
Shahid Kapoor's Cocktail 2 has powered to a Rs 76.25 crore global box office haul in just three days, cementing its status as one of Bollywood's stronger openings of the year. The figures, tracked through Day 3, reflect healthy footfalls across domestic multiplexes and a solid overseas showing from Indian diaspora markets.

The original Cocktail, released in 2012, was a turning point in Kapoor's career, and its sequel has evidently carried that brand equity into a new decade. Early audience response appears to have driven strong word-of-mouth over the opening weekend, with collections building rather than declining sharply — a pattern that augurs well for the film's total run.

For the Hindi film industry, the number matters beyond just this one title. Several mid-range productions have struggled to find traction in theatres since the industry reset post-pandemic, and a clear Rs 76-crore global weekend from a single-hero commercial film reinforces the case for theatrical investment at scale.

The coming weekdays will be the real test: whether Cocktail 2 sustains or drops sharply will determine whether it crosses the crucial Rs 150-crore mark that typically defines a solid commercial success for a film at this budget level.
key_facts: Cocktail 2 grosses Rs 76.25 crore globally in first three days
Shahid Kapoor leads the film
Sequel to the 2012 hit Cocktail

stories.headline: 'Peek In, Peek Out': New Documentary Brings Delhi's Khirki Village Migrant Story to Screen
stories.what: A new film titled Peek In, Peek Out, set in Delhi's Khirki village, brings the story of India's internal migrants to the fore, according to The Indian Express. Khirki, a dense urban village in South Delhi, has long been a transit and settlement point for migrants from across India and parts of Africa, making it a microcosm of the country's complex urban demographic shifts. The film's release marks a rare instance of Indian documentary or indie filmmaking training its lens on an often-overlooked neighbourhood and its residents.
stories.lens: India's internal migration story is one of the defining socioeconomic realities of our time, and films that humanise it beyond statistics fill a genuine gap in the national cultural conversation.
stories.article:
A new film, Peek In, Peek Out, centres on Delhi's Khirki village — one of the capital's most densely layered urban settlements — and brings the lived experience of India's internal migrants into cinematic focus. The Indian Express spotlights the film as a significant contribution to the small but growing body of Indian work that takes urban migration as its primary subject.

Khirki village, nestled in South Delhi's Malviya Nagar area, is a place of extraordinary demographic complexity. It has historically absorbed waves of migrants from Rajasthan, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh and Northeast India, as well as a notable community of African residents — students, traders and workers — whose presence has at various points generated both cultural richness and social tension.

By rooting a film in this specific geography, Peek In, Peek Out attempts something Indian mainstream cinema rarely does: treating the migrant experience not as backdrop or comic relief but as the central narrative. The intergenerational dimension — how families who arrived in Khirki a generation ago have shaped the village, and how new arrivals navigate the same labyrinthine lanes — offers rich material for documentary or narrative treatment.

For Indian indie filmmakers, Khirki represents a goldmine of stories that national media and mainstream entertainment have largely bypassed. The film's reception will be a test of whether such hyperlocal, migration-focused storytelling can find the audiences — on OTT platforms or the festival circuit — that it deserves.
key_facts: Film titled Peek In, Peek Out set in Delhi's Khirki village
Khirki is known as a hub for internal migrants from across India
Film brings migrant experiences and intergenerational stories to screen

stories.headline: Ten Korean Dramas Are Dominating Indian Streaming Right Now
stories.what: A new India Today report highlights that ten Korean dramas are currently among the most-watched titles on streaming platforms in India, reflecting the sustained and deepening grip of the Korean Wave — or Hallyu — on Indian entertainment consumption. The phenomenon, which accelerated sharply post-pandemic with titles like Squid Game and Crash Landing on You, has now matured into a consistent content category that Indian streaming services actively programme for. The trend has significant implications for both Korean content exports and Indian platform strategy.
stories.lens: Korean dramas pulling in Indian viewers at scale means Indian streaming platforms are dedicating real shelf space and subtitling budgets to foreign content — reshaping what 'mainstream' Indian entertainment looks like.
stories.article:
Ten Korean dramas are currently capturing the most Indian streaming attention, according to India Today, in a sign that the Korean Wave is not a passing fad but a settled part of how urban — and increasingly small-town — India consumes entertainment.

The Hallyu phenomenon first found serious Indian footing during the pandemic years, when locked-down viewers discovered binge-friendly Korean series on Netflix and other platforms. What began as curiosity has hardened into habit: dedicated fan communities, dubbed and subtitled versions, and active social media fandoms have all taken root across India's metro and tier-2 cities.

For Indian streaming platforms, the numbers carry clear strategic weight. Investing in Korean content licensing and localised subtitling delivers a disproportionate return because Korean dramas tend to inspire intense loyalty and repeat viewing — audiences who finish one series almost invariably seek out another. This creates a predictable, self-sustaining audience segment that platforms can rely on.

The broader cultural implication is also notable: Indian viewers, traditionally assumed to prefer domestic content, are demonstrating a growing appetite for well-produced foreign drama regardless of language. That shift is forcing Indian producers and OTT executives to rethink assumptions about what Indian audiences will and won't watch — a conversation that will shape commissioning decisions for years to come.
key_facts: Ten Korean dramas currently among most-watched on Indian streaming platforms
Trend reflects deepening of Hallyu (Korean Wave) in India
Phenomenon accelerated post-pandemic with titles like Squid Game

stories.headline: Mukesh Khanna Collaborates With Samay Raina After Publicly Calling for Him to Be 'Paraded on a Donkey'
stories.what: Mukesh Khanna, best known for playing Shaktimaan in the iconic Doordarshan series, has collaborated with stand-up comedian Samay Raina — despite having previously and very publicly declared that Raina 'should be paraded on a donkey' for content he found disrespectful. The about-turn is striking given the vehemence of Khanna's earlier criticism, and has set off considerable chatter about the nature of celebrity feuds and reconciliations in the social media age. The collaboration was reported by The Indian Express.
stories.lens: The Khanna-Raina patch-up is a snapshot of how quickly public feuds between old-guard TV icons and new-age digital comedians dissolve when both sides see mutual benefit — a dynamic that defines Bollywood and entertainment PR today.
stories.article:
Mukesh Khanna, the actor who became a household name across India as the caped superhero Shaktimaan in Doordarshan's landmark 1990s series, has now chosen to work with Samay Raina — the same comedian he once said 'should be paraded on a donkey' for content Khanna considered disrespectful to his legacy and to Indian cultural values.

The original outburst had made headlines precisely because of its colourful ferocity: Khanna is known for outspoken views on contemporary entertainment, and his condemnation of Raina had been unambiguous. Raina, for his part, has built a substantial following on YouTube and Instagram with roast-style comedy that frequently targets Bollywood celebrities and nostalgia culture.

The collaboration — the details of which were not fully specified in the report — signals either a genuine reconciliation, a calculated PR move by both parties, or simply the pragmatic logic of Indian entertainment in 2026, where reach and virality often override past friction. Raina's digital audience is enormous, and Khanna's nostalgia value among millennial viewers is considerable; the overlap is commercially obvious.

For fans of both, the pairing is equal parts surprising and entertaining. It also underscores a broader trend: India's old-media icons and new-media creators are increasingly finding common ground, recognising that cross-generational content tends to perform well across both television and streaming platforms.
key_facts: Mukesh Khanna previously called for Samay Raina to be 'paraded on a donkey'
Khanna is best known as Shaktimaan from Doordarshan's 1990s series
Despite past public feud, both have now collaborated

stories.headline: Why Agent Vinod Is Sriram Raghavan's Most Fascinating Failure
stories.what: The Indian Express has published a retrospective examination of Agent Vinod, the 2012 Saif Ali Khan spy thriller directed by Sriram Raghavan, arguing that it remains his most fascinating failure — a film that overreached its own ambitions but in doing so revealed something distinctive about Raghavan's filmmaking sensibility. Raghavan, who went on to craft the widely acclaimed Andhadhun (2018) and Merry Christmas (2024), has always operated at the intersection of pulp and precision. The piece revisits why Agent Vinod fell short commercially and critically despite its director's evident craft.
stories.lens: Reassessing a director's misfires is how Indian film culture develops critical depth — and for a filmmaker of Raghavan's stature, understanding Agent Vinod illuminates everything he made after it.
stories.article:
Sriram Raghavan's Agent Vinod, released in 2012 with Saif Ali Khan in the lead, has long occupied an awkward place in Hindi cinema's memory: too slick to dismiss, too sprawling to entirely defend. The Indian Express revisits the film and makes a case for it being Raghavan's most revealing work precisely because of how it fails.

Raghavan is a filmmaker with a cult following built on meticulous noir plotting — his Ek Hasina Thi, Johnny Gaddaar and eventually the blockbuster Andhadhun all demonstrate a director who thinks deeply about genre mechanics. Agent Vinod was his most expensive and ambitious project up to that point, a pan-continental spy thriller that attempted to do James Bond on a Bollywood budget, with stops across Russia, Morocco and Afghanistan.

Where it struggled was in reconciling Raghavan's instinct for tight, character-driven suspense with the demands of a big-budget commercial entertainer that needed item numbers, comedy tracks and a romance arc. The seams showed. Commercially, the film underperformed against its considerable budget and was considered a setback for both Khan and the director.

But as the Indian Express argues, the film's failures are instructive: they show a director stress-testing the limits of his style, learning what happens when the constraints of a personal genre sensibility collide with the machinery of mainstream Bollywood production. Everything Raghavan has made since — including the dazzling Andhadhun — bears the marks of lessons learned from Agent Vinod's overreach.
key_facts: Agent Vinod released in 2012, directed by Sriram Raghavan, starring Saif Ali Khan
Raghavan later directed the acclaimed Andhadhun (2018)
Film considered a commercial and critical underperformer despite evident craft
